title: switch Statement
description: Branching on a fixed value with switch: case, break, default, and the classic fallthrough bug.
objectives: Write a switch statement with multiple case labels
Understand why break is needed after each case
Understand what fallthrough is and why it's dangerous
Know when switch is a better fit than a long else if chain

sections.text: This works, but it repeats day == over and over just to compare the same variable against different fixed values. switch is built exactly for this pattern: matching one value against several fixed possibilities.

sections.title: The pieces
points: case option: — one possible match; runs if value equals option
break; — stops the switch from continuing past this case
default: — optional fallback, like else, for when nothing matched

sections.title: Tracing it
points: break; stops execution here — case 1 and case 2 are skipped entirely, and default is never reached

sections.text: Without break;, execution doesn't stop at the matched case — it "falls through" and keeps running every case after it, until it hits a break or the end of the switch. Here, day = 1 matches case 1, prints Monday, and then keeps going straight into case 2, printing Tuesday too. Output: MondayTuesday, not just Monday. Forgetting break is one of the most common switch bugs.

Fallthrough isn't always a bug — stacking case 6: directly above case 7: with no code or break between them is a common, deliberate way to say "either of these matches runs the same code." The danger is only when a break is missing by accident.
Just like else, default is optional. If it's omitted and value matches no case, the switch simply does nothing and execution continues after it.

rows: Matches one value against several fixed options
Can test any condition, including ranges and complex expressions
No fallthrough concept — each branch is independent
Reads cleanly for many discrete cases
Reads cleanly for a small number of varied conditions

sections.text: switch cannot check ranges like marks >= 80 directly — it only compares for exact equality against fixed values. The grade calculator from earlier lessons needs else if for that reason; something like matching an exact day number is a better fit for switch.

sections.title: A vending machine
sections.text: Press button 1, get a soda. Press button 2, get chips. Press button 3, get candy. Press anything else, nothing happens (or an error message). That's exactly a switch: one input, matched against several exact fixed options, with a default for anything unexpected.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting break; after a case — execution falls through into the next case's code instead of stopping.
Trying to use a range in a case, like case day >= 1 && day <= 5: — switch only supports exact-value matches, not conditions.
Forgetting default — leaving unmatched values silently doing nothing, when a fallback message might be expected.
Using switch when the conditions aren't simple equality checks — a long else if chain is the right tool for ranges or complex logic.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a switch statement used for?
questions.answer: Branching based on matching one value against several fixed, exact possibilities, as a cleaner alternative to a long chain of == comparisons in else if.

questions.question: What happens if break is omitted in a switch case?
questions.answer: Execution falls through into the next case's code and keeps running until it hits a break or the end of the switch — a common source of bugs.

questions.question: What does default do in a switch?
questions.answer: It's an optional fallback branch that runs when the value didn't match any of the case labels, similar to else in an if chain.

questions.question: Can a switch statement check a range like age >= 18?
questions.answer: No — switch only supports exact-value matching against a case label; ranges and complex conditions require if / else if.

questions.question: Is fallthrough always a bug?
questions.answer: No — stacking case labels with no code between them is a common intentional pattern for making multiple values share the same code. It's only a bug when a break is missing by accident.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: switch matches one value against several fixed case labels, running the matching case's code. Each case should end with break; to stop execution there — omitting it causes fallthrough, where execution keeps running into the following cases, a classic switch bug. default is an optional fallback for values that match no case, similar to else. Unlike if / else if, switch only supports exact-value equality checks, not ranges or complex conditions, but it reads more cleanly when there are many discrete fixed options to match.

sections.title: Trick to Remember switch
sections.text: switch = a vending machine. Each case is a button. break = stop after this button's item comes out. Forget break, and it keeps dispensing every item after it too.
